2. **The COPY semantics diverge — this is the real fork.**
   - e01 copies once to `<ENSHRINES_ROOT>/<ANCHOR>:<ANCHOR>` (anchor as both package and tag — *peer packages*), then stops (e01:72-80).
   - l01 copies to `<LODES_ROOT>/<STAMP>:<DIGEST_TAG>` (digest-tag), then **retags** with two more member tags via a GAR→GAR loop using `--src-creds`+`--dest-creds` (l01:88-102). e01 never retags and never uses `--src-creds`.
   So the *destination URI construction* and the *retag loop* are not shared. Only the leading `skopeo copy --all docker://ORIGIN docker://DEST --dest-creds oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}` line (l01:88-92 ≈ e01:76-80) is structurally identical.
3. **Substitution namespace** — `_RBGL_*` (with `TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX`, `TAG_BOLE`, `LODES_ROOT`) vs `_RBGE_*` (with `ENSHRINES_ROOT`, `ENSHRINES_CATEGORY`). l01 has digest-prefix and bole-tag concepts e01 lacks entirely.
4. **Envelope/result tail is fully per-kind** (the members[] envelope in l01:110-137 vs the flat locator JSON in e01:84-91) — confirmed out-of-scope per the task framing; stays per-kind.

**Parameterization needed to make Snippet B authored-once:** the snippet that is *genuinely* common is exactly: `inspect --raw → sha256sum → SANITIZED → SHORT → first skopeo copy --all to a destination` — where the destination URI is passed in as a parameter and the *retag loop is excluded* (it's Lode-only). The fingerprint/anchor name is the same formula and can be emitted by the snippet as a named output var. Everything past the first copy (retag loop, member tags, envelope) is per-kind tail.

---

## Finding 3 — Identical vs superficially-similar, ranked sharpest-first

| Block | Sites | Verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metadata TOKEN fetch** | l01, e01 | **CLEAN** — verbatim, no params | l01:27-33 ≡ e01:21-27, byte-identical, no substitutions |
| **buildx builder bootstrap** | l02, v03 | **CLEAN** — verbatim 3 lines | l02:27-29 ≡ v03:46-48, byte-identical |
| **inspect→sha256→sanitize→short-hex preamble** | l01, e01 | **REFINEMENT** — share if raw-file path is a param; fingerprint formula identical | l01:65-78 ≈ e01:49-62; only var labels differ |
| **first `skopeo copy --all ... --dest-creds`** | l01, e01 | **REFINEMENT** — share if dest URI is a param | l01:88-92 ≈ e01:76-80 |
| **JSON result accumulator (`RESULT='{'`/`FIRST`/comma-join)** | l01, e01, **i01** | **REFINEMENT** — identical *idiom*, different payload | l01:51/132-137, e01:36-37/86-91, i01:31-32/59-64 — the `if FIRST … else RESULT="${RESULT},"` join is verbatim across three; only the inner object differs |
| **3-slot ORIGIN unrolling + `for SLOT in 1 2 3`** | l01, e01 | **REFINEMENT** — same skeleton; l01 also carries STAMP | l01:37-61 vs e01:31-45; l01 has the extra STAMP slot array |
| **vouch-push step (whole)** | l02 vs v03 | **BLOCKER** — see Finding 1 | divergence on cardinality/URI-arity/platform/Dockerfile |
| **skopeo retag loop** | l01 only | **n/a** — Lode-exclusive, not shared | l01:95-102; absent in e01 |
| **per-platform conditional Dockerfile** | v03 only | **n/a** — hallmark-exclusive | v03:36-44; absent in l02 |
| **MANIFEST docker-pull/tag/push** | i01 only | **n/a** — kind-specific, excluded by task | i01:22-65 |

---

## Summary verdicts

- **(A) Vouch-push unification (l02⊕v03): BLOCKER.** The shared core is a 3-line buildx idiom; everything else (loop cardinality, URI arity, platform-literal-vs-file, conditional Dockerfile, disjoint substitution namespaces) is load-bearing. Unification injects four new branch points into a behavior-preserving heat to delete ~15 lines — a net complexity increase. Recommend: extract only the **buildx bootstrap** as a shared snippet (CLEAN); leave l02 and v03 as distinct steps.

- **(B) Composed snippets (token fetch + skopeo block): split verdict.**
  - Token fetch: **CLEAN** — author once, drop into l01/e01 verbatim.
  - skopeo inspect/digest/fingerprint preamble + first copy: **REFINEMENT** — shareable if (i) raw-file temp path is a parameter and (ii) destination URI is a parameter, with the snippet emitting `SHA`/`SHORT`/`SANITIZED`/fingerprint as named outputs. The **retag loop and member-tag construction stay Lode-only**; the **envelope/result tail stays per-kind** (already accepted).

- **Bonus CLEAN-ish candidate (REFINEMENT):** the `RESULT='{' … FIRST/comma-join … /builder/outputs/output` accumulator idiom is verbatim across **three** capture steps (l01, e01, i01) — a wider sharing opportunity than the task scoped, if the heat wants it. Only the inner per-slot object differs.

## Under-specified items to flag for the pace

1. **Unified substitution vocabulary** — any sharing across `_RBGL_`/`_RBGE_`/`_RBGV_`/`_RBGN_` requires deciding whether snippets read host-injected substitutions or pure shell-var parameters. The token-fetch snippet is param-free (CLEAN); every other candidate touches a kind-specific substitution namespace, so the contract (does the snippet see `_RBGx_*` or only positional shell vars set by the host before inlining?) is undecided and gates everything in Finding 2's REFINEMENT row.
2. **Where the snippet boundary cuts the first skopeo copy** — l01's first copy targets a *digest-tag* URI that the retag loop then consumes; e01's first copy targets the *final anchor* URI. If the shared snippet ends at "first copy," l01 still needs its retag tail and e01 needs nothing more — confirm the snippet's output contract is "image is now at DEST under this tag," leaving downstream retag to the per-kind tail.
3. **Platform parameter mode for any future buildx sharing** — if the buildx bootstrap is shared but the `--platform` arg is not, confirm that's the intended seam (literal for Lode, file-read for hallmark stays caller-side).

No files were modified; this is read-only analysis. Scripts examined: the five paths listed in the task, all read in full.

content.text:
I now have enough to assess all four findings precisely. Let me verify one last load-bearing point: whether the four named build primitives are truly kindle-independent (callable from a new spine module without the rbld/rbfc kindle chain entanglement). I saw `zrbfc_write_script_body` and `zrbfc_native_path_capture` carry no sentinel; `zrbfc_wait_build_completion` and `zrbfc_ensure_git_metadata` call `zrbfc_sentinel`. Let me confirm the wait poller's dependencies on module-level constants that the spine would need to inherit.

I have all the evidence needed. Let me compile the assessment.

---

## PRE-IMPLEMENTATION REVIEW: Capture-Assembly Spine (₣BX, pace capture-spine-data-driven)

I read the full ensconce path in `rbld_Lode.sh` and the build primitives in `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`, then cross-checked the four other `_submit` siblings (`zrbfl_inscribe_submit`, `zrbfd_enshrine_submit`, `zrbfd_mirror_submit`, `zrbfv_about/vouch/graft_submit`) because the spine's value depends on whether this skeleton is genuinely shared. It is — there are 5-7 near-twin copies. That changes the verdict character: the boundary is real and carvable, but several design points are under-specified.

Sharpest first.

---

### Finding 1 — The recipe model is sound, but "ordered step IDs + one substitutions blob" undersells what the step-defs array already carries. REFINEMENT (capture-spine-data-driven)

**Evidence.** Steps today are assembled from a `|`-delimited step-def array (`rbld_Lode.sh:93-96`), not positionally:
```
z_step_defs=(
  "rbgjl01-ensconce-capture.sh|${z_rbfc_tool_skopeo}|ensconce-capture"
  "rbgjl02-assemble-push-vouch.sh|${z_rbfc_tool_docker}|assemble-push-vouch"
)
```
Each row is `script | builder-image | id`. The about/vouch siblings (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:486-491`, `558-562`) carry a **fourth** column, `entrypoint` (`bash`/`sh`/`python3`), which selects the shebang baked in front of the body (`:525-531`). So a "recipe row" is already first-class and ordered — but its real shape is `(script_path, builder_image, id, entrypoint)`, not a bare step id. A recipe of *ids alone* would be an indirection that loses the script-path → builder → entrypoint binding the loop needs.

**So the recipe is carvable, but the implementer must decide the row schema.** Two facts constrain it:
- The builder image (`z_rbfc_tool_skopeo`, `z_rbfc_tool_docker`) is resolved from the reliquary into mutable kindle state (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:137-152`). If the spine owns no kind knowledge, the **body** must hand it resolved builder refs per row — the spine cannot look them up.
- The ensconce loop bakes a literal `#!/bin/bash` shebang (`rbld_Lode.sh:125`), while the shared about/vouch assembler is entrypoint-driven. The spine needs the entrypoint column to subsume both, or it silently regresses the about/vouch path it presumably also wants to absorb.

**Decision the implementer must make before starting:** is a recipe row `(script_path, builder_image, id, entrypoint)` handed fully-resolved by the body? If yes, this is a clean data model. If the spine is expected to take *ids* and resolve scripts/builders itself, that is a logic ADD that re-imports kind knowledge — contradicting the spine's no-kind-knowledge mandate.

---

### Finding 2 — The substitutions blob is the cleanest seam; the extract/capture-file logic is the dirtiest. REFINEMENT (capture-spine-data-driven)

**Substitutions (clean).** Every sibling builds the build JSON with the identical `jq -n` envelope: `steps`, `substitutions`, `serviceAccount`, `options{automapSubstitutions,logging,pool}`, `timeout` (`rbld_Lode.sh:160-186`; `rbfl:127-142`; `rbfd:1018-1043`). The **only** kind-specific content is the inner key/value set of `substitutions` (`_RBGL_*` for lode, `_RBGN_*` for inscribe, `_RBGE_/_RBGR_*` for enshrine). These keys are produced host-side from kind constants and consumed verbatim by the step scripts (confirmed: `rbgjl/*.sh` reference exactly `_RBGL_GAR_HOST`, `_RBGL_LODE_1_STAMP`, etc.). So "substitutions = an opaque pre-built blob the body hands the spine" maps **perfectly** — the spine slots `$blob` into the envelope and never reads a key. This is the strongest part of the proposal.

**Extract (interwoven).** `zrbld_ensconce_extract` (`rbld_Lode.sh:214-252`) is where generic and kind logic are threaded together in one function:
- *Generic:* `jq -r '.results.buildStepOutputs[N]'` → base64-decode → non-empty check (`:222-228`). Identical to `zrbfd_enshrine_extract_anchors:1082-1088`. The **only generic parameter is the step index N** (0 for ensconce, 1 for enshrine — because enshrine prepends a preflight step).
- *Kind:* the `slot_1/2/3` loop, `.vouch` envelope extraction, `buf_write_fact_multi "${z_stamp}" "${RBCC_fact_ext_lode}"`, and the `bole`-specific success line (`:238-251`). This is pure body knowledge.

**The named "extract buildStepOutputs into capture-files" spine operation cleanly carves to exactly:** *decode buildStepOutputs[N] to a file, hand that file to the body.* The per-slot envelope/stamp/fact-multi loop is body, not spine. The boundary is clean **once you cut it at the decoded-output-file**, but the current single function does not cut there — so this is a real carve with a defined cut line, not a relocation. The index N is the one generic parameter the body must supply.

---

### Finding 3 — The four named build primitives are present and callable, with one kindle-coupling caveat. CLEAN→REFINEMENT (capture-spine-data-driven)

All four exist in `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`:

| Primitive | Function | Sentinel-coupled? |
|---|---|---|
| write-script-body | `zrbfc_write_script_body` (`:422`) | **No** — pure builtins, no `zrbfc_sentinel`. Comment explicitly notes it "carries no sentinel and stays unit-testable without the foundry kindle chain" (`:447-450`). Cleanly relocatable as `rbfcb_`. |
| native-path | `zrbfc_native_path_capture` (`:451`) | **No** — reads only its arg + `BURD_OSTYPE`. Same kindle-independence note. Clean. |
| git-metadata | `zrbfc_ensure_git_metadata` (`:381`) | **Yes** — calls `zrbfc_sentinel` and writes to kindle constants `ZRBFC_GIT_*_FILE`. |
| wait-build-completion | `zrbfc_wait_build_completion` (`:249`) | **Yes** — calls `zrbfc_sentinel`; depends on kindle constants `ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE`, `ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL`, `ZRBFC_BUILD_STATUS_FILE`, `ZRBFC_POLL_*`, `ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_INTERVAL_SEC/RETRY_TOLERANCE`, and `RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE`. |

**Caveat the implementer must resolve:** the docket says the spine is "built on relocated Foundry-core build primitives (`rbfcb_`)." Two of the four (write-script-body, native-path) are already kindle-free and relocate trivially. The other two are **wired into rbfc kindle state** — `wait-build-completion` in particular reads 8+ `ZRBFC_*` constants and the poll-ceiling policy table (`:116-124`). Relocating these to `rbfcb_` means either (a) moving those constants/policy with them, or (b) parameterizing them. The poll-ceiling per build-kind (`ENSCONCE`, `INSCRIBE`, `CONJURE`…) is itself kind-flavored data — ensconce passes `ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_CEILING_ENSHRINE` (`rbld_Lode.sh:207`, note: ensconce reuses the *enshrine* ceiling). The spine should take the ceiling as a recipe/body parameter, not own the table. This is a design decision, not a blocker — but it's the part that's more than "relocation + light wiring."

---

### Finding 4 — No blocker: the spine is a real factoring of an already-duplicated skeleton, not new logic. CLEAN (capture-spine-data-driven)

**Evidence that the boundary is genuine, not invented.** The submit skeleton repeats verbatim across 5+ functions. Every one does, in order: construct GAR host/path/mason-SA (3 identical lines) → assemble steps into a JSON array via the write-body/escape/jq-append loop → compose build JSON with the identical 6-key envelope → `rbrd_check` → `rbuh_json POST` → extract `.metadata.build.id` → persist to `ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE` → emit console link → `zrbfc_wait_build_completion`. Lines `rbld_Lode.sh:191-207` are **byte-near-identical** to `rbfl:147-163` and `rbfd:1048-1064`. This block is pure spine with zero kind knowledge — it is the most carvable code in the set.

The kind-specific residue that stays in the body is small and well-localized:
- host-side stamp mint (`rbld_Lode.sh:311`)
- the substitutions key/value set (the blob)
- the step-def rows (script/builder/id/entrypoint)
- the post-extract envelope/fact-multi loop
- the poll ceiling + extract index N

Nothing in the kind residue is *interleaved* with the submit skeleton in a way that blocks separation — it's all either upstream (data handed in) or downstream (output file handed back). **There is no BLOCKER.** The "design plus carve" framing holds.

---

### Summary for the implementer

| # | Verdict | What must be decided before starting |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | **CLEAN** | Submit skeleton is duplicated 5+ times, zero kind knowledge — pure carve. |
| 2 (subs) | **CLEAN** | Substitutions as opaque body-built blob maps perfectly; spine never reads a key. |
| 1 | **REFINEMENT** | Recipe row schema: confirm it's `(script_path, builder_image, id, entrypoint)` handed pre-resolved by the body, NOT bare ids the spine resolves (the latter re-imports kind knowledge). |
| 2 (extract) | **REFINEMENT** | Cut the extract at the decoded-output-file. Spine = decode `buildStepOutputs[N]`; body = per-slot envelope/stamp/`buf_write_fact_multi` loop. N is the one generic param. |
| 3 | **REFINEMENT** | `write-script-body` + `native-path` relocate clean (already kindle-free). `wait-build-completion` + `git-metadata` are wired to `ZRBFC_*` kindle constants + the poll-ceiling policy table; decide whether to move the constants or parameterize. Poll ceiling should be a recipe/body parameter (ensconce already borrows the enshrine ceiling).

**One cross-cutting opportunity to flag:** the spine, if cut at these seams, would also subsume `inscribe`, `enshrine`, `mirror`, `about`, `vouch`, and `graft` submits — they share the identical skeleton. The docket scopes only the bole ensconce carve; whether the spine is validated against just ensconce or designed to absorb the siblings is a scope decision the operator should make explicitly, since it changes how general the recipe-row and substitution-blob contracts must be. The about/vouch path is the stress test (4 steps, entrypoint column, git-metadata, 4KB substitution guards) — if the recipe model survives that, it survives everything.

**Relevant files:**
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbld_Lode.sh` (ensconce path: submit `:79-208`, extract `:214-252`, driver `:257-318`)
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` (primitives: wait `:249`, git `:381`, write-body `:422`, native-path `:451`; step assemblers `:478`/`:550`/`:621`; poll-ceiling table `:116-124`)
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:69` (inscribe submit — closest single-step twin)
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:943` (enshrine submit + extract — the other buildStepOutputs case, index 1)
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:420` (about submit — the multi-step/entrypoint/git-metadata stress case)
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbgjl/` (ensconce step scripts consuming `_RBGL_*` substitutions)

I changed nothing, ran nothing, and edited nothing — read-only review as instructed.

The spine is to be "Built on relocated Foundry-core build primitives (rbfcb_): write-script-body, wait-build-completion, native-path, git-metadata."

YOUR TASK: Assess whether this spine/body boundary is concretely carvable from the code as it exists, and whether the recipe+substitutions data model has a real workable shape against what the bole ensconce submit does TODAY. Read:
- /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbld_Lode.sh  (focus the ensconce path — find the submit/poll/extract function(s), likely named like zrbld_ensconce_submit or similar)
- /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh  (find the build-host primitives the spine will call: the function that writes a step script body, the build-completion poller, native-path, git-metadata; and any step-assembly that builds the Cloud Build JSON)

Report, with concrete evidence (function names, signatures, how data flows):
1. Trace the current bole ensconce submit end to end: how is the Cloud Build request JSON constructed? How are step scripts put into it (inlined? referenced files? heredoc? a write-script-body helper)? How are substitutions passed? How does it poll? How does it extract buildStepOutputs into capture-files?
2. Where is bole-KIND knowledge (stamps, members[] envelope, ensconce-specific fields) entangled with the GENERIC submit/poll/extract machinery? Is the boundary clean (kind logic already separable) or interwoven (kind details threaded through the submit code)?
3. Does "recipe = ordered step ids + substitutions blob" map onto how steps are currently assembled? Are step ids already first-class, or are steps assembled positionally/implicitly such that a recipe-of-ids needs a new indirection (and is that indirection a logic ADD beyond pure carve)?
4. Are the named build primitives (write-script-body, wait-build-completion, native-path, git-metadata) actually present and cleanly callable, or are they entangled with surrounding kindle/state?

Verdict per finding: CLEAN (carvable as relocation+light wiring), REFINEMENT (carvable but a design decision is under-specified — name it), or BLOCKER (the boundary is not cleanly carvable / requires substantial new logic, contradicting "design plus carve"). Tag each to the spine pace (capture-spine-data-driven). Sharpest first. Note anything an implementer must decide before starting.

# Pre-Implementation Review: Can the rbld / rbfc / rbfl splits be done with zero logic edits?

**Verdict in one line:** The split is achievable as pure relocation for the *function bodies*, because bash sourcing flattens everything into one global namespace — but **the single `zrbfc_kindle` / sentinel / multiple-inclusion-guard scaffolding cannot be mechanically copied into 6 files without becoming wrong**. That scaffolding is the one place a deliberate decision (a REFINEMENT) is forced, and one sub-case of it is a true BLOCKER if done naively. Sharpest first.

---

## FINDING 1 — The single `zrbfc_kindle` setting ~40 `readonly` consts consumed by all 6 clusters — REFINEMENT (with a BLOCKER trap)

**This is the load-bearing finding.** `zrbfc_kindle()` (rbfc lines 30–127) is one function that establishes the entire module's `readonly` state in one shot: `ZRBFC_GCB_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_PACKAGE_BASE`, `ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE`, `ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE`, `ZRBFC_PACKAGE_LIST_FILE`, the `ZRBFC_RBGJ{A,V,R}_STEPS_DIR` step dirs, the `ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_CEILING_*` budgets, and more.

Every proposed cluster reads these:
- `rbfcg_` (GAR-REST: `zrbfc_list_packages_capture`, `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact`) reads `ZRBFC_GAR_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_PACKAGE_BASE`, `ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES`.
- `rbfcp_` (plumb) reads `ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE` dozens of times (every `jq ... > "${ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE}"` in the show-sections code, lines 1064–1527).
- `rbfca_` (step-assembly) reads `ZRBFC_RBGJA_STEPS_DIR`, `ZRBFC_RBGJV_STEPS_DIR`, `ZRBFC_RBGJR_STEPS_DIR` and the `z_rbfc_tool_*` vars.
- `rbfcb_` (build-host) reads the `ZRBFC_BUILD_*` poll files/budgets and `ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL`.

**Why it still works mechanically:** Sourcing is flat. As long as `zrbfc_kindle()` runs once (in the same shell) before any consumer is *called*, the `readonly` consts are global and every cluster sees them — regardless of which file the consumer's body now lives in. The CLI callers (`rbfc_cli.sh:66`, `rbfl_cli.sh:76`, `rbld_cli.sh:76`, `rbfd:40`, etc.) all invoke `zrbfc_kindle` once at furnish time, then call publics. Function *definition* site and *call* site being in different files is irrelevant to bash. **So if `zrbfc_kindle` stays whole and lands in exactly one cluster (the proposed `rbfck_` kindle/shared file), the consts move freely.** That is the clean path.

**Where it becomes a decision (REFINEMENT):** The split's own cluster letters imply distribution of *ownership*. The `BASH_SOURCE`-relative step-dir derivations in kindle —

```
local z_self_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"          # rbfc:94
readonly ZRBFC_RBGJA_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgja"
```

— are conceptually "step-assembly" state (`rbfca_`), but they're set inside `zrbfc_kindle`, which the plan puts in `rbfck_`. The implementer must decide: **does the whole kindle body stay monolithic in `rbfck_`, or do the per-cluster consts migrate to per-cluster kindle helpers?** The first is pure relocation; the second is a re-architecture (= logic edit). The discipline ("zero logic edits") only survives if the answer is "kindle stays whole in one file." This needs to be *cinched before the explosion starts*, because the cluster names suggest the wrong (re-architecting) answer.

**The BLOCKER trap inside this finding:** If an implementer reads "6 files, each needs its own kindle/sentinel/guard" (as Q1 and Q5 of the task hint) and *splits the kindle* so each file readonly-sets its own slice, that is a real logic edit and it will also **double-readonly-fault** on any shared const — and worse, `zrbfc_kindle` is the single idempotency gate (`test -z "${ZRBFC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"`, line 31). You cannot have six kindle functions each guarding on `ZRBFC_KINDLED`. **The only zero-logic-edit answer is: the kindle function, the `zrbfc_sentinel`, and the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` flag are themselves indivisible and must all land together in one cluster file.** Treat the kindle/sentinel/guard trio as an atom, not as something to distribute.

---

## FINDING 2 — `z_rbfc_tool_*` global mutable state set in one cluster, read in three others — CLEAN relocation, but ONLY because of an undocumented global-leak; flag it

The six `z_rbfc_tool_gcloud/docker/alpine/syft/binfmt/skopeo` vars are the sharpest *correctness* coupling across the proposed cluster boundary, and they are subtle.

- **Written** in `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images()` (rbfc 145–150) — proposed `rbfcv_` (vessel-resolution) or `rbfca_`.
- **Initialized** (to `""`) inside `zrbfc_kindle` (rbfc 103–108) — proposed `rbfck_`.
- **Read** in `zrbfc_assemble_about_steps` (487–490, 523), `zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps` (559–561), `zrbfc_assemble_preflight_step` (646) — proposed `rbfca_`.
- **Read across the module boundary** in `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lines 522–531, 589, 667, 975, 1492) — a *different file entirely*, today.

Critical evidence: these vars are assigned **without `local`, without `declare -g`, without `readonly`** — at lines 103–108 and 145–150 they are bare assignments inside functions. That means they leak to the global shell scope. **This already works cross-file today** (rbfd reads them after calling `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images`), which is the proof that the cross-cluster read will also work after the split: bash global scope does not care which file the writer and reader live in.

**Classification: CLEAN.** The relocation does not break this. **But flag it loudly to the implementer:** the correctness here rests entirely on an implicit global-leak, not on any explicit declaration. The current code comments call it "mutable kindle state" (rbfc 102, 135) but nothing *enforces* globalness. An implementer "tidying while relocating" who adds a `local` or `readonly` to these — a natural instinct when a function moves to a new file and looks unowned — would silently break conjure/about/vouch step assembly across the whole foundry. **The instruction must be: copy these six lines byte-for-byte, do not annotate them.** This is exactly the kind of "logic edit riding along" the discipline forbids, and it's invisible unless called out.

---

## FINDING 3 — Cross-cluster private-function calls — CLEAN (sourcing makes them global)

Every `zrbfc_*` private helper becomes a global function the moment its file is sourced, so calls across cluster files resolve fine. The dense cross-cluster call graph (all currently intra-file, all becoming inter-file):

| Caller (proposed cluster) | Callee (proposed cluster) | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `rbfc_vessel_for_hallmark_capture` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:946 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `rbfc_require_vessel_sigil` (rbfcv_) | rbfc:950 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `zrbfc_load_vessel` (rbfcv_) | rbfc:954 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:972, 981 |
| `rbfc_vessel_for_hallmark_capture` (rbfcg_) | `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:914 — intra-cluster, fine |
| `zrbfc_assemble_*` (rbfca_) | `zrbfc_write_script_body` (rbfcb_/rbfck_) | rbfc:517, 588, 635 |
| `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images` (rbfcv_) | `zrbfc_sentinel` (rbfck_) | rbfc:138 — every function calls the sentinel |
| `zrbld_ensconce_submit` (rblds_) | `zrbfc_write_script_body`, `zrbfc_wait_build_completion` (rbfc) | rbld:120, 207 — cross-*module*, already works |
| `rbld_divine` (rbldl_) | `zrbfc_list_anchors_capture` (rbfc) | rbld:338 |
| `rbfl_inscribe` (inscribe cluster) | `zrbfc_wait_build_completion`, `zrbfc_write_script_body` (rbfc) | rbfl:91, 163 |

Note especially: **every single function in all three files calls a sentinel as its first line** (`zrbfc_sentinel` / `zrbfl_sentinel` / `zrbld_sentinel`). After the split, every cluster file's functions call into the kindle-cluster's sentinel. This is fine (global), but it confirms Finding 1's atom rule: the sentinel must exist and must have run-readonly `ZRBFC_KINDLED` before *any* clustered function runs.

None of these helpers rely on file-local state (bash has no file-local scope; only function-local `local` vars, which never escape a function regardless of file). **Classification: CLEAN across the board.**

---

## FINDING 4 — `BASH_SOURCE[0]`-relative path derivations — CLEAN if same dir, BLOCKER if any cluster goes to a subdir

There are four `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` derivations across the three files:

- rbfc:94 — `z_self_dir` → `ZRBFC_RBGJA/V/R_STEPS_DIR` (resolves to `Tools/rbk/rbgja` etc.)
- rbld:31 — `source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh"`
- rbld:51 — `ZRBLD_RBGJL_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgjl"`
- rbfl:29, 45 — `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` and `ZRBFL_RBGJI_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgji"`

`${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` resolves to *the directory of the file the line physically lives in*. **As long as every new cluster file lands in the same `Tools/rbk/` directory as the original (which the proposed `rbfcv_`/`rbfcg_`/`rbld{k,l,s,b}_` naming implies — flat siblings), these resolve identically. CLEAN — confirmed.**

**BLOCKER condition:** if any cluster file is placed in a *subdirectory* (e.g. `Tools/rbk/rbfc/rbfcp_...`), then `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` in that file resolves to `Tools/rbk/rbfc`, and the step-dir derivations (`/rbgja`, `/rbgjl`, `/rbgji`) and the `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` lines would point at the wrong directory — silently for the step dirs (caught only at runtime by the `test -d ... || buc_die`), and immediately for the source line. The clustering description says "smaller prefixed files under" the dir, which reads as flat siblings, but **this must be confirmed explicitly: all cluster files stay flat in `Tools/rbk/`.** If the implementer is tempted to nest them, the `BASH_SOURCE`-relative lines become a forced logic edit — i.e., a BLOCKER for the discipline.

---

## FINDING 5 — Multiple-inclusion guards: two different idioms, neither survives naive per-file replication — REFINEMENT

The two guard idioms behave differently and this matters for the split:

**rbfc uses silent-skip:** `test -z "${ZRBFC_SOURCED:-}" || return 0` (rbfc:24). This is deliberate — rbfc is sourced by *many* children (rbld, rbfl, rbfk, rbfr, rbfv, rbfd, and all their CLIs — confirmed: 11 source sites). The silent skip lets re-sourcing be a harmless no-op.

**rbld/rbfl use die-on-re-source:** `test -z "${ZRBLD_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "multiply sourced"` (rbld:27, rbfl:25). These are leaf modules sourced exactly once.

The task's framing — "how does that interact with 6 new files each needing its own guard?" — points at the actual decision. **A naive split that gives each of the 6 rbfc cluster files its own `ZRBFCx_SOURCED` guard is a logic edit** (new guard variables = new state = not a relocation), and it would also change behavior: today rbfc is one source unit with one guard; six units with six guards changes the multiply-sourced semantics six-fold.

**The zero-logic-edit answer:** keep **one** umbrella source unit. The natural structure is a single sourced entry file (the kindle/shared cluster, `rbfck_`) that carries the *one* `ZRBFC_SOURCED` silent-skip guard and `source`s its five sibling cluster files; the five body files carry **no** guard (they are only ever reached through the entry file). That preserves the exact current external contract: every existing `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` site keeps working if `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` becomes that entry file (or the entry file keeps that name). **This is a REFINEMENT — movable, but the "where does the guard live and who sources whom" question must be decided before splitting, and it is currently under-specified.**

Same logic applies to rbld (4 files) and rbfl (clustered): the `ZRBLD_SOURCED` / `ZRBFL_SOURCED` die-guard must remain on a single entry file, with the body clusters guard-free and pulled in by that entry.

---

## Under-specified items an implementer must have decided BEFORE starting

1. **Kindle/sentinel/guard is an indivisible atom.** `zrbfc_kindle`, `zrbfc_sentinel`, the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` flag, and the `ZRBFC_SOURCED` guard must all land in *one* cluster file, undivided. The cluster letters (`rbfck_` = kindle/shared) suggest this, but the readonly consts that kindle sets are consumed by *other* clusters — the implementer must not be tempted to migrate const definitions to the clusters that read them. **Decide and cinch: kindle stays whole.** (Finding 1)

2. **Entry-file topology.** One file per module retains the inclusion guard and `source`s the sibling body clusters; body clusters are guard-free. Which file keeps the canonical name (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`) so the 11 existing `source` sites and the Rust test driver (`rbtdrf_fast.rs:1874` sources `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` by name) don't break? **The original filename must survive as the entry file, or every source site is a ride-along edit.** (Finding 5)

3. **Flat vs nested placement.** All cluster files must be flat siblings in `Tools/rbk/` or the `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` step-dir and cross-source lines silently/loudly break. (Finding 4)

4. **`z_rbfc_tool_*` copied verbatim, un-annotated.** No `local`, no `readonly`, no `declare` added during the move. Their global-leak is load-bearing and undeclared. (Finding 2)

5. **External-consumer awareness.** `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` reads `z_rbfc_tool_*` and calls `zrbfc_assemble_*` / `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images` from outside. The split's internal cluster boundaries are invisible to rbfd *as long as the entry file still pulls in all clusters*. Verify the entry file sources every body cluster, or rbfd loses helpers it currently gets transitively.

---

## Bottom line

- **The function bodies move freely** — bash's flat global namespace makes every cross-cluster private call and every cross-cluster const/global read work identically after the split (Findings 2, 3). No body needs a logic edit.
- **The scaffolding does not split.** Kindle, sentinel, the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` idempotency flag, and the `ZRBFC_SOURCED` inclusion guard are a single atom that must land in one cluster file, with an entry-file topology sourcing the rest (Findings 1, 5). Getting this wrong is the one place the "zero logic edits" discipline gets violated — either by distributing the kindle (re-architecture), by minting per-file guards (new state), or by adding declarations to the tool-vars (Finding 2). All three are tempting precisely *because* the file is being split.
- **No hard BLOCKER exists** *if* the four decisions above are cinched up front. The BLOCKERs are all conditional — they only materialize if the implementer nests files in a subdir (Finding 4) or tries to make the kindle/guard genuinely per-cluster (Finding 1). The relocation is feasible as pure relocation; the risk is entirely in the kindle/guard/global-leak scaffolding, which must be handled as copy-verbatim atoms rather than as code to redistribute.

Files: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`, `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh`, `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbld_Lode.sh`; consumers to keep working: `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`, `rbfc_cli.sh`, `rbfl_cli.sh`, `rbld_cli.sh`, `rbfk_kludge.sh`, `rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh`, `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh`, `rbfh_cli.sh`, and `rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`.

---

## FINDING 1 — The single `zrbfc_kindle` setting ~40 `readonly` consts consumed by all 6 clusters — REFINEMENT (with a BLOCKER trap)

**This is the load-bearing finding.** `zrbfc_kindle()` (rbfc lines 30–127) is one function that establishes the entire module's `readonly` state in one shot: `ZRBFC_GCB_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_PACKAGE_BASE`, `ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE`, `ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE`, `ZRBFC_PACKAGE_LIST_FILE`, the `ZRBFC_RBGJ{A,V,R}_STEPS_DIR` step dirs, the `ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_CEILING_*` budgets, and more.

Every proposed cluster reads these:
- `rbfcg_` (GAR-REST: `zrbfc_list_packages_capture`, `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact`) reads `ZRBFC_GAR_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_GAR_PACKAGE_BASE`, `ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE`, `ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES`.
- `rbfcp_` (plumb) reads `ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE` dozens of times (every `jq ... > "${ZRBFC_SCRATCH_FILE}"` in the show-sections code, lines 1064–1527).
- `rbfca_` (step-assembly) reads `ZRBFC_RBGJA_STEPS_DIR`, `ZRBFC_RBGJV_STEPS_DIR`, `ZRBFC_RBGJR_STEPS_DIR` and the `z_rbfc_tool_*` vars.
- `rbfcb_` (build-host) reads the `ZRBFC_BUILD_*` poll files/budgets and `ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL`.

**Why it still works mechanically:** Sourcing is flat. As long as `zrbfc_kindle()` runs once (in the same shell) before any consumer is *called*, the `readonly` consts are global and every cluster sees them — regardless of which file the consumer's body now lives in. The CLI callers (`rbfc_cli.sh:66`, `rbfl_cli.sh:76`, `rbld_cli.sh:76`, `rbfd:40`, etc.) all invoke `zrbfc_kindle` once at furnish time, then call publics. Function *definition* site and *call* site being in different files is irrelevant to bash. **So if `zrbfc_kindle` stays whole and lands in exactly one cluster (the proposed `rbfck_` kindle/shared file), the consts move freely.** That is the clean path.

**Where it becomes a decision (REFINEMENT):** The split's own cluster letters imply distribution of *ownership*. The `BASH_SOURCE`-relative step-dir derivations in kindle —

```
local z_self_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"          # rbfc:94
readonly ZRBFC_RBGJA_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgja"
```

— are conceptually "step-assembly" state (`rbfca_`), but they're set inside `zrbfc_kindle`, which the plan puts in `rbfck_`. The implementer must decide: **does the whole kindle body stay monolithic in `rbfck_`, or do the per-cluster consts migrate to per-cluster kindle helpers?** The first is pure relocation; the second is a re-architecture (= logic edit). The discipline ("zero logic edits") only survives if the answer is "kindle stays whole in one file." This needs to be *cinched before the explosion starts*, because the cluster names suggest the wrong (re-architecting) answer.

**The BLOCKER trap inside this finding:** If an implementer reads "6 files, each needs its own kindle/sentinel/guard" (as Q1 and Q5 of the task hint) and *splits the kindle* so each file readonly-sets its own slice, that is a real logic edit and it will also **double-readonly-fault** on any shared const — and worse, `zrbfc_kindle` is the single idempotency gate (`test -z "${ZRBFC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"`, line 31). You cannot have six kindle functions each guarding on `ZRBFC_KINDLED`. **The only zero-logic-edit answer is: the kindle function, the `zrbfc_sentinel`, and the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` flag are themselves indivisible and must all land together in one cluster file.** Treat the kindle/sentinel/guard trio as an atom, not as something to distribute.

---

## FINDING 2 — `z_rbfc_tool_*` global mutable state set in one cluster, read in three others — CLEAN relocation, but ONLY because of an undocumented global-leak; flag it

The six `z_rbfc_tool_gcloud/docker/alpine/syft/binfmt/skopeo` vars are the sharpest *correctness* coupling across the proposed cluster boundary, and they are subtle.

- **Written** in `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images()` (rbfc 145–150) — proposed `rbfcv_` (vessel-resolution) or `rbfca_`.
- **Initialized** (to `""`) inside `zrbfc_kindle` (rbfc 103–108) — proposed `rbfck_`.
- **Read** in `zrbfc_assemble_about_steps` (487–490, 523), `zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps` (559–561), `zrbfc_assemble_preflight_step` (646) — proposed `rbfca_`.
- **Read across the module boundary** in `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lines 522–531, 589, 667, 975, 1492) — a *different file entirely*, today.

Critical evidence: these vars are assigned **without `local`, without `declare -g`, without `readonly`** — at lines 103–108 and 145–150 they are bare assignments inside functions. That means they leak to the global shell scope. **This already works cross-file today** (rbfd reads them after calling `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images`), which is the proof that the cross-cluster read will also work after the split: bash global scope does not care which file the writer and reader live in.

**Classification: CLEAN.** The relocation does not break this. **But flag it loudly to the implementer:** the correctness here rests entirely on an implicit global-leak, not on any explicit declaration. The current code comments call it "mutable kindle state" (rbfc 102, 135) but nothing *enforces* globalness. An implementer "tidying while relocating" who adds a `local` or `readonly` to these — a natural instinct when a function moves to a new file and looks unowned — would silently break conjure/about/vouch step assembly across the whole foundry. **The instruction must be: copy these six lines byte-for-byte, do not annotate them.** This is exactly the kind of "logic edit riding along" the discipline forbids, and it's invisible unless called out.

---

## FINDING 3 — Cross-cluster private-function calls — CLEAN (sourcing makes them global)

Every `zrbfc_*` private helper becomes a global function the moment its file is sourced, so calls across cluster files resolve fine. The dense cross-cluster call graph (all currently intra-file, all becoming inter-file):

| Caller (proposed cluster) | Callee (proposed cluster) | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `rbfc_vessel_for_hallmark_capture` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:946 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `rbfc_require_vessel_sigil` (rbfcv_) | rbfc:950 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `zrbfc_load_vessel` (rbfcv_) | rbfc:954 |
| `zrbfc_plumb_core` (rbfcp_) | `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:972, 981 |
| `rbfc_vessel_for_hallmark_capture` (rbfcg_) | `zrbfc_gar_extract_artifact` (rbfcg_) | rbfc:914 — intra-cluster, fine |
| `zrbfc_assemble_*` (rbfca_) | `zrbfc_write_script_body` (rbfcb_/rbfck_) | rbfc:517, 588, 635 |
| `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images` (rbfcv_) | `zrbfc_sentinel` (rbfck_) | rbfc:138 — every function calls the sentinel |
| `zrbld_ensconce_submit` (rblds_) | `zrbfc_write_script_body`, `zrbfc_wait_build_completion` (rbfc) | rbld:120, 207 — cross-*module*, already works |
| `rbld_divine` (rbldl_) | `zrbfc_list_anchors_capture` (rbfc) | rbld:338 |
| `rbfl_inscribe` (inscribe cluster) | `zrbfc_wait_build_completion`, `zrbfc_write_script_body` (rbfc) | rbfl:91, 163 |

Note especially: **every single function in all three files calls a sentinel as its first line** (`zrbfc_sentinel` / `zrbfl_sentinel` / `zrbld_sentinel`). After the split, every cluster file's functions call into the kindle-cluster's sentinel. This is fine (global), but it confirms Finding 1's atom rule: the sentinel must exist and must have run-readonly `ZRBFC_KINDLED` before *any* clustered function runs.

None of these helpers rely on file-local state (bash has no file-local scope; only function-local `local` vars, which never escape a function regardless of file). **Classification: CLEAN across the board.**

---

## FINDING 4 — `BASH_SOURCE[0]`-relative path derivations — CLEAN if same dir, BLOCKER if any cluster goes to a subdir

There are four `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` derivations across the three files:

- rbfc:94 — `z_self_dir` → `ZRBFC_RBGJA/V/R_STEPS_DIR` (resolves to `Tools/rbk/rbgja` etc.)
- rbld:31 — `source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh"`
- rbld:51 — `ZRBLD_RBGJL_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgjl"`
- rbfl:29, 45 — `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` and `ZRBFL_RBGJI_STEPS_DIR="${z_self_dir}/rbgji"`

`${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` resolves to *the directory of the file the line physically lives in*. **As long as every new cluster file lands in the same `Tools/rbk/` directory as the original (which the proposed `rbfcv_`/`rbfcg_`/`rbld{k,l,s,b}_` naming implies — flat siblings), these resolve identically. CLEAN — confirmed.**

**BLOCKER condition:** if any cluster file is placed in a *subdirectory* (e.g. `Tools/rbk/rbfc/rbfcp_...`), then `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` in that file resolves to `Tools/rbk/rbfc`, and the step-dir derivations (`/rbgja`, `/rbgjl`, `/rbgji`) and the `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` lines would point at the wrong directory — silently for the step dirs (caught only at runtime by the `test -d ... || buc_die`), and immediately for the source line. The clustering description says "smaller prefixed files under" the dir, which reads as flat siblings, but **this must be confirmed explicitly: all cluster files stay flat in `Tools/rbk/`.** If the implementer is tempted to nest them, the `BASH_SOURCE`-relative lines become a forced logic edit — i.e., a BLOCKER for the discipline.

---

## FINDING 5 — Multiple-inclusion guards: two different idioms, neither survives naive per-file replication — REFINEMENT

The two guard idioms behave differently and this matters for the split:

**rbfc uses silent-skip:** `test -z "${ZRBFC_SOURCED:-}" || return 0` (rbfc:24). This is deliberate — rbfc is sourced by *many* children (rbld, rbfl, rbfk, rbfr, rbfv, rbfd, and all their CLIs — confirmed: 11 source sites). The silent skip lets re-sourcing be a harmless no-op.

**rbld/rbfl use die-on-re-source:** `test -z "${ZRBLD_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "multiply sourced"` (rbld:27, rbfl:25). These are leaf modules sourced exactly once.

The task's framing — "how does that interact with 6 new files each needing its own guard?" — points at the actual decision. **A naive split that gives each of the 6 rbfc cluster files its own `ZRBFCx_SOURCED` guard is a logic edit** (new guard variables = new state = not a relocation), and it would also change behavior: today rbfc is one source unit with one guard; six units with six guards changes the multiply-sourced semantics six-fold.

**The zero-logic-edit answer:** keep **one** umbrella source unit. The natural structure is a single sourced entry file (the kindle/shared cluster, `rbfck_`) that carries the *one* `ZRBFC_SOURCED` silent-skip guard and `source`s its five sibling cluster files; the five body files carry **no** guard (they are only ever reached through the entry file). That preserves the exact current external contract: every existing `source .../rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` site keeps working if `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` becomes that entry file (or the entry file keeps that name). **This is a REFINEMENT — movable, but the "where does the guard live and who sources whom" question must be decided before splitting, and it is currently under-specified.**

Same logic applies to rbld (4 files) and rbfl (clustered): the `ZRBLD_SOURCED` / `ZRBFL_SOURCED` die-guard must remain on a single entry file, with the body clusters guard-free and pulled in by that entry.

---

## Under-specified items an implementer must have decided BEFORE starting

1. **Kindle/sentinel/guard is an indivisible atom.** `zrbfc_kindle`, `zrbfc_sentinel`, the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` flag, and the `ZRBFC_SOURCED` guard must all land in *one* cluster file, undivided. The cluster letters (`rbfck_` = kindle/shared) suggest this, but the readonly consts that kindle sets are consumed by *other* clusters — the implementer must not be tempted to migrate const definitions to the clusters that read them. **Decide and cinch: kindle stays whole.** (Finding 1)

2. **Entry-file topology.** One file per module retains the inclusion guard and `source`s the sibling body clusters; body clusters are guard-free. Which file keeps the canonical name (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`) so the 11 existing `source` sites and the Rust test driver (`rbtdrf_fast.rs:1874` sources `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` by name) don't break? **The original filename must survive as the entry file, or every source site is a ride-along edit.** (Finding 5)

3. **Flat vs nested placement.** All cluster files must be flat siblings in `Tools/rbk/` or the `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` step-dir and cross-source lines silently/loudly break. (Finding 4)

4. **`z_rbfc_tool_*` copied verbatim, un-annotated.** No `local`, no `readonly`, no `declare` added during the move. Their global-leak is load-bearing and undeclared. (Finding 2)

5. **External-consumer awareness.** `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` reads `z_rbfc_tool_*` and calls `zrbfc_assemble_*` / `zrbfc_resolve_tool_images` from outside. The split's internal cluster boundaries are invisible to rbfd *as long as the entry file still pulls in all clusters*. Verify the entry file sources every body cluster, or rbfd loses helpers it currently gets transitively.

---

## Bottom line

- **The function bodies move freely** — bash's flat global namespace makes every cross-cluster private call and every cross-cluster const/global read work identically after the split (Findings 2, 3). No body needs a logic edit.
- **The scaffolding does not split.** Kindle, sentinel, the `ZRBFC_KINDLED` idempotency flag, and the `ZRBFC_SOURCED` inclusion guard are a single atom that must land in one cluster file, with an entry-file topology sourcing the rest (Findings 1, 5). Getting this wrong is the one place the "zero logic edits" discipline gets violated — either by distributing the kindle (re-architecture), by minting per-file guards (new state), or by adding declarations to the tool-vars (Finding 2). All three are tempting precisely *because* the file is being split.
- **No hard BLOCKER exists** *if* the four decisions above are cinched up front. The BLOCKERs are all conditional — they only materialize if the implementer nests files in a subdir (Finding 4) or tries to make the kindle/guard genuinely per-cluster (Finding 1). The relocation is feasible as pure relocation; the risk is entirely in the kindle/guard/global-leak scaffolding, which must be handled as copy-verbatim atoms rather than as code to redistribute.

Files: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`, `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh`, `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbld_Lode.sh`; consumers to keep working: `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`, `rbfc_cli.sh`, `rbfl_cli.sh`, `rbld_cli.sh`, `rbfk_kludge.sh`, `rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh`, `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh`, `rbfh_cli.sh`, and `rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`.

content.text:
Four deep reads came back, and they're rich. I've weighed them, pushed back where a subagent overstated, and added the cross-pace ordering analysis myself. Here's my synthesis — and a direct answer to your question.

## Bottom line

The peer chat's repair was sound and the heat is **mostly executable** — but the implementation read surfaced real things planning couldn't see. So yes, a **modest round of decisions is needed before execution**, concentrated in two places: one cinch worth reconsidering (₢BXAAE vouch-push), and one design decision that no pace currently owns (the relocation topology). Everything else is docket-sharpening, and one item gets *cheaper*. No finding kills the heat; its spine is genuinely sound.

## Prioritized — sharpest first

**① BLOCKER (decision-gated, not infeasible) — ₢BXAAE `cloud-step-library`: the "unify the one whole shared step" cinch is a net-complexity loss.**
The paddock cinches vouch-push as "the genuinely-identical whole step (rbgjl02 + rbgjv03)." A precise diff says it isn't: they diverge on **five load-bearing axes** — cardinality (loop-over-N stamps vs single artifact), URI arity (hallmark has an extra path segment + tag-is-the-hallmark vs a fixed `_TAG_VOUCH`), platform (hardcoded `linux/amd64` vs read-from-file multi-arch with fail-fast), conditional Dockerfile (`verify-*.json` branch vs fixed two-line), and disjoint substitution namespaces. The **only** verbatim-shared block is a 3-line `buildx` bootstrap. Unifying would *inject* ~4 new branch points to delete ~15 lines — the Load-Bearing Complexity principle inverted. It's not mechanically infeasible, but as cinched it's counterproductive by the heat's own rule. **Decision needed before ₢BXAAE runs:** keep the cinch (accept the branching) or revise to "share the buildx bootstrap; leave l02/v03 distinct." This is the one item that touches a cinch — your call / the design chat's, not mine to settle. Relatedly: ₢BXAAE's *other* mechanism (composed snippets) has its cleanest shared block — the metadata-token fetch — shared between bole capture (l01) and **enshrine (e01), which is the not-migrated fork** — so in-heat that snippet has one live consumer. It's forward-investment (consistent with the paddock's "partial dedup" admission), not present dedup. **Net: ₢BXAAE's dedup thesis is weaker on both mechanisms than the paddock states.**

**② REFINEMENT (prerequisite gap) — ₢BXAAB / ₢BXAAC `*-module-explosion`: the "mechanical" relocation depends on a non-mechanical topology decision that no pace owns.**
Function *bodies* move freely (bash's flat namespace), so pure relocation is feasible — **but** the kindle/sentinel/sourced-guard scaffolding does *not* split. Concretely, before these "agent-choreographed, mechanical" paces run, someone must cinch:
- **Kindle is an indivisible atom** — `zrbfc_kindle` + `zrbfc_sentinel` + the single `ZRBFC_KINDLED` idempotency flag + the `ZRBFC_SOURCED` guard land *whole* in one entry cluster (naturally `rbfck_`), which sources the 5 guard-free body clusters. Splitting the kindle per-file (which the paddock's "a sourced-guard always" line subtly invites) double-`readonly`-faults and breaks the single idempotency gate.
- **`z_rbfc_tool_*` globals copied byte-for-byte, un-annotated** — they leak to global scope by bare assignment (no `local`/`readonly`/`declare`); rbfd reads them cross-file *today*. A reflexive "tidy" that adds `local` while moving them silently breaks conjure/about/vouch assembly. This is the textbook "logic edit riding along."
- **Flat placement only** — every `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` step-dir derivation breaks if a cluster is nested in a subdir.
- **Call-site rewrites are forced and cross-language** — terminal exclusivity (₢BXAAA's mint: `rbfc` stops naming a thing) *requires* deleting `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` and repointing all ~11 source sites **including the Rust test driver `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, which sources `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` by name.** The dockets say "all call sites updated" — make sure that explicitly includes the Rust driver and the out-of-scope external consumer `rbfd` (which reaches into rbfc internals).

None of this is a blocker, but the explosion paces aren't truly "mechanical" until it's cinched. **Recommend a short topology-cinch (fold into ₢BXAAA or a pre-explosion note).**

**③ REFINEMENT — ₢BXAAD `capture-spine-data-driven`: the recipe model is under-described.** The docket says "recipe = ordered step ids + substitutions blob." The code says a step row is really **`(script_path, builder_image, id, entrypoint)`** — the about/vouch siblings carry a 4th `entrypoint` column (bash/sh/python3) the ensconce loop hardcodes. A recipe of *bare ids* would force the spine to resolve scripts/builders/entrypoints itself — which re-imports the kind knowledge the spine is forbidden to hold. **Decide: the body hands the spine fully-resolved rows.** (The substitutions-blob half, by contrast, is the cleanest seam in the whole heat — the spine slots an opaque blob into an identical `jq` envelope and never reads a key.)

**④ REFINEMENT — ₢BXAAD: the extract has a real cut-line, and two build primitives are kindle-wired.** `zrbld_ensconce_extract` threads generic + kind together; the clean cut is **spine = decode `buildStepOutputs[N]` to a file; body = the per-slot envelope/stamp/`buf_write_fact_multi` loop**, with `N` the one generic parameter. And of the four named primitives: `write-script-body` + `native-path` are already kindle-free (clean relocate to `rbfcb_`); `wait-build-completion` + `git-metadata` are wired to 8+ `ZRBFC_*` constants and a per-kind **poll-ceiling table** (ensconce notably *borrows the enshrine ceiling*). Decide: move the constants with them, or parameterize — the poll-ceiling wants to be a body/recipe parameter, not spine-owned. This confirms ₢BXAAD is genuinely "design + carve," not relocation.

**⑤ REFINEMENT — ₢BXAAE: composed-snippet contract is undecided.** Does a snippet read host-injected `_RBGx_*` substitutions, or only positional shell vars the host sets before inlining? The token-fetch snippet is param-free (CLEAN, verbatim across l01/e01); the skopeo inspect→sha256→fingerprint→first-copy block is shareable *only if* raw-file path and destination URI become parameters, with the Lode-only retag loop and per-kind envelope staying out. This contract gates every non-trivial snippet.

**⑥ REFINEMENT (scope) — ₢BXAAD: how general is the spine?** The submit skeleton is duplicated **5–7 times** (ensconce, inscribe, enshrine, mirror, about, vouch, graft) — so the spine is a *real* factoring, not invented. But the docket validates it against bole ensconce only. Decide whether to design the contract to absorb the siblings (the 4-step about/vouch path with the entrypoint column + git-metadata is the stress test) or just prove it on ensconce. Operator call; it changes how general the recipe-row contract must be.

**⑦ CLEAN / simplification — ₢BXAAJ `provenance-insensitivity-spike`: no cook needed.** The verifier gates *only* on DSSE keyid, openssl signature validity, and `builder.id == GoogleHostedWorker` — all builder-identity/input. The one recipe-ish field (`buildDefinition.buildType`) is **read but never asserted**; nothing reads `steps`/`buildConfig`/`externalParameters`. Restructuring steps changes none of the three gates → verdict invariant. The code-read is **decisive**; ₢BXAAJ can drop its "one cook if ambiguous" contingency and become a pure read. (Honest Pale caveat: this vouches what *our* verifier asserts, not that Google's upstream signing is step-insensitive — but since we gate on identity+signature, our verdict holds regardless.)

## What's confirmed sound (no work needed)
- The **spine carve** is a true factoring of an already-duplicated skeleton — ₢BXAAD's core is real.
- The **relocation** is feasible as pure relocation — bodies move freely; risk is entirely in the scaffolding (item ②).
- The **behavior-preserving rail holds** at the provenance layer — confirmed from source.
- ₢BXAAF (validator), ₢BXAAG (spec), ₢BXAAH (gate), ₢BXAAI (CBG) raised nothing.

## So — improvements, and a little more work
The repair direction was right; the dockets are honest now. Before execution I'd want three things settled, none large: **(a)** the ₢BXAAE vouch-push cinch decision (the sharpest — and the design chat owns it), **(b)** a topology cinch for the explosions (item ②), and **(c)** the ₢BXAAD recipe-row + extract sharpenings (③/④). ₢BXAAJ gets simpler, not harder.

I changed no pace and wrote no code, per the brief. Want me to turn this into reslate-ready docket deltas for ₢BXAAD/₢BXAAE (and a topology note), or hold it as review notes for the design chat to fold in?

# jjezs_reslate BXAAB

## Character
Mechanical, agent-choreographed. Relocation only — no smuggling.

## Goal
Extract the boundary-independent slices of rbld into their own files — lifecycle REST (rbldl_), kindle/shared-consts (rbldk_) — plus the capture-relevant Foundry build-primitives (rbfcb_). The spine/body carve (rblds_/rbldb_) is NOT here: that boundary is designed in the spine pace, so it does not get pre-split mechanically.

## Cinched (paddock)
- Relocation only: zero logic edits ride along; lode-lifecycle is the oracle.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- Kindle/sentinel/guard is one indivisible atom — do NOT distribute it: rbldk_ is the entry file holding rbld's single guard + kindle and sourcing its guard-free siblings; cluster files stay flat in Tools/rbk/ (BASH_SOURCE-relative paths break if nested).
- rbfcb_ keeps sourcing the kindle for the ZRBFC_ constants its primitives read — pure relocation; making any primitive kindle-independent for unit-testing is a separate anneal, not this move.

## Execution
Per-file sonnet agents (each handed the old module + its slice); review the set, then remove relocated content; haiku for call-site rewrites.

## Done
- rbldl_, rbldk_, rbfcb_ exist; their content is removed from the monoliths; all call sites updated; lode-lifecycle green.
# jjezs_reslate BXAAC

## Character
Mechanical, broad, agent-choreographed. Relocation only — no smuggling.

## Goal
Decompose the rest of rbfc and rbfl into their rbfcX_/rbflX_ clusters per the paddock legend (rbfcb_ already extracted in the lode-extraction pace). Reaches made-side files (step-assembly, plumb); acceptable as pure relocation.

## Cinched (paddock)
- Relocation only: a move carries no logic change.
- Inner-loop oracle is shellcheck + the fast/service fixtures plus one conjure path; the FULL skirmish is NOT re-run here — the milestone gate carries it.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- Kindle/sentinel/guard is one indivisible atom: rbfck_ becomes the entry file holding rbfc's single guard + whole kindle and sourcing the guard-free body clusters; flat in Tools/rbk/ only.
- Copy the leaked tool-image globals (z_rbfc_tool_*) verbatim — global-by-bare-assignment, read cross-module by rbfd; adding local/readonly/declare while moving them silently breaks step assembly.
- Call-site rewrites are forced and cross-language: terminal exclusivity deletes rbfc_FoundryCore.sh, so all source sites AND the Rust test driver (rbtdrf_fast.rs, sources it by name) repoint; rbfd reads rbfc internals, so the entry file must keep sourcing the full cluster set.

## Execution
Per-file sonnet agents; review the set, then delete the old files; haiku for call-site rewrites.

## Done
- rbfc and rbfl decomposed into their clusters; old files deleted after review; all call sites updated; the targeted fixtures green.
# jjezs_reslate BXAAD

## Character
The heat's core — design plus implementation.

## Goal
Design the spine/body boundary and the recipe + substitutions contract shape, then carve rblds_ (spine) and rbldb_ (bole body) accordingly and make the spine data-driven; bole rides it.

## Cinched (paddock)
- Registration-agnostic: the spine takes the recipe as data — no per-kind branch, no kind-registration or discovery mechanism; registering kinds is left to the consumer, not baked into the spine.
- The spine/body boundary is decided HERE, not pre-split mechanically at the lode explosion; the requires/provides contract FORMAT is designed here against the capture domain's known axes (N-member envelopes, tool-variety, opaque-blob payloads) and recorded in the contract's RBSCJ section.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- A recipe row is (script_path, builder_image, id, entrypoint) handed pre-resolved by the body — NOT bare step ids the spine resolves (resolving would re-import kind knowledge). The substitutions blob stays opaque to the spine: it slots into an identical jq envelope, reads no key.
- Extract cut-line: the spine decodes buildStepOutputs[N] to a file (N the one generic param); the per-slot envelope / stamp / buf_write_fact_multi loop is body. Today's zrbld_ensconce_extract threads both — this is a real carve, not relocation.
- The poll ceiling is kind-flavored (ensconce borrows the enshrine ceiling); the body/recipe passes it — the spine does not own the ceiling table.

## Done
- rblds_/rbldb_ carved along the designed boundary; bole ensconce composes through the spine via recipe + substitutions, with no kind knowledge in the spine; behavior-preserving (lode-lifecycle green, provenance verdicts identical).
# jjezs_reslate BXAAE

## Character
Extraction touching one made-side step; mints the shared-cloud-step home.

## Goal
Stand up the shared cloud step-library: mint its home namespace (which breaks the rbgj family-letter scheme, since a shared step belongs to no single family), then unify the FROM-scratch vouch-push across Lode (rbgjl02) and hallmark-verify (rbgjv03); author the metadata-token fetch and the skopeo copy/digest block as composed snippets; keep envelope authoring per-kind. Each library step declares its requires/provides, refining the contract shape from the spine pace.

## Cinched (paddock)
- The shared cloud-step home is minted HERE (the library pace) — it breaks the rbgj family-letter scheme since a shared step belongs to no single family.
- vouch-push is the one whole shared step (two live callers); rbgjv03 is the only made-side touch.
- Shared in-step logic rides the composed-snippet channel; the members[] envelope tail stays per-kind.
- The ensconce collision guard is out of scope (behavior-adding); this pace neither adds it nor forecloses it — keep the capture step's pre-copy point clean.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- OPEN, resolve before executing: the vouch-push "one whole shared step" claim is challenged — l02 vs v03 diverge on five load-bearing axes (loop-N vs single, URI arity, platform literal-vs-file, conditional Dockerfile, disjoint _RBGL_/_RBGV_ substitutions); only a 3-line buildx bootstrap is verbatim-shared, so unifying as cinched injects ~4 branches to delete ~15 lines. Settle keep-vs-share-bootstrap-only (a paddock decision) first.
- Composed-snippet contract to decide: the metadata-token fetch is verbatim-shareable (param-free); the skopeo inspect→digest→fingerprint→first-copy block shares only if raw-file path + destination URI are parameters (the Lode-only retag loop and the per-kind envelope stay out). Decide whether a snippet reads host-injected _RBGx_ substitutions or only shell vars the host sets before inlining.
- Snippet payoff is mostly forward: the token/skopeo block's other consumer is enshrine (e01), which is NOT migrated — so in-heat the snippet has one live caller (bole). Consistent with the paddock's partial-dedup, but state it.

## Done
- The shared-cloud-step home is minted and recorded; the Lode and hallmark vouch-push paths ride one library step; token + copy/digest composed once; each step's requires/provides declared; lode-lifecycle and a conjure path green with verdicts identical to pre-refactor.
# jjezs_reslate BXAAJ

## Character
Cheap de-risk of the heat's load-bearing assumption — gates the spine/library investment.

## Goal
Confirm SLSA provenance survives cloud-step restructuring BEFORE investing in the spine and library, by reading the verifier (rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py).

## Cinched (paddock)
- The behavior-preserving rail rests on this; if it is false, the spine/library approach changes — so it gates them, rather than being discovered at the final gate.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- Verdict reached, NO cook needed: the verifier gates only on DSSE keyid, openssl signature validity, and builder.id == GoogleHostedWorker — all builder-identity. The one recipe-ish field (buildDefinition.buildType) is read but never asserted; nothing reads steps/buildConfig/externalParameters. Restructuring steps leaves the verdict invariant; the conjure read is decisive.
- This review already performed the read, so mounting AAJ is confirm-and-record, not fresh investigation. Pale caveat: this vouches what our verifier asserts, not Google's upstream signing — but we gate on identity+signature, so our verdict holds regardless.

## Done
- The verdict (provenance insensitive to step restructuring) is recorded so the spine and library proceed on confirmed ground.

Mechanical, broad, agent-choreographed. Relocation only — no smuggling.

## Goal
Decompose the rest of rbfc and rbfl into their rbfcX_/rbflX_ clusters per the paddock legend (rbfcb_ already extracted in the lode-extraction pace). Reaches made-side files (step-assembly, plumb); acceptable as pure relocation.

## Cinched (paddock)
- Relocation only: a move carries no logic change.
- Inner-loop oracle is shellcheck + the fast/service fixtures plus one conjure path; the FULL skirmish is NOT re-run here — the milestone gate carries it, so the heat pays one full cook, not two.

## Found (pre-impl review)
- Kindle/sentinel/guard is one indivisible atom: rbfck_ becomes the entry file holding rbfc's single guard + whole kindle and sourcing the guard-free body clusters; flat in Tools/rbk/ only.
- Copy the leaked tool-image globals (z_rbfc_tool_*) verbatim — global-by-bare-assignment, read cross-module by rbfd; adding local/readonly/declare while moving them silently breaks step assembly.
- Call-site rewrites are forced and cross-language: terminal exclusivity deletes rbfc_FoundryCore.sh, so all source sites AND the Rust test driver (rbtdrf_fast.rs, sources it by name) repoint; rbfd reads rbfc internals, so the entry file must keep sourcing the full cluster set.

## Execution
Per-file sonnet agents; review the set, then delete the old files; haiku for call-site rewrites.

## Done
- rbfc and rbfl decomposed into their clusters, matching their acronym entries; old files deleted after review; all call sites updated, and sibling context docs naming the old modules swept so nothing points at a deleted file; the targeted fixtures green.
